figures.name: Cyaxares
figures.significance: Destroyed the Assyrian Empire in alliance with Babylon

name: Achaemenid Empire
note: Used Aramaic as the administrative lingua franca but Persian was the dynastic identity; Cyrus was notably religiously tolerant

policies.name: Cyrus's Policy of Religious Tolerance
policies.description: Conquered peoples kept their religions — including releasing Jews from Babylonian captivity. Famous policy; the empire remained Zoroastrian in dynastic identity.

figures.name: Cyrus the Great
figures.significance: Founded the largest empire yet seen; issued the Cyrus Cylinder; freed the Jews from Babylonian captivity

figures.name: Darius I
figures.significance: Built Persepolis; reorganized the empire into satrapies

figures.name: Xerxes I
figures.significance: Led the great Persian invasion of Greece (Thermopylae, Salamis)

name: Parthian Empire (Arsacid)
note: Highly decentralized; Hellenized early, then reverted to Iranian/Zoroastrian identity

figures.name: Arsaces I
figures.significance: Founded the Arsacid dynasty; began the Parthian revolt against the Seleucids

figures.name: Mithridates I
figures.significance: Captured Mesopotamia from the Seleucids; turned Parthia into a true empire

name: Sassanid Empire
note: Zoroastrianism as explicit state religion; Magi priestly class wielded significant power

figures.name: Ardashir I
figures.significance: Overthrew the Parthians; revived Persian and Zoroastrian identity

figures.name: Shapur I
figures.significance: Captured Roman Emperor Valerian — the only Roman emperor taken prisoner by a foreign enemy

figures.name: Khosrow I (Anushirvan)
figures.significance: The ideal Persian king; reformed taxation; translated Greek philosophy into Pahlavi

name: Ilkhanate
note: Started Tengriist/Buddhist; converted to Sunni Islam and were fully Persianized — Persian remained the language of court and literature

figures.name: Hulagu Khan
figures.significance: Sacked Baghdad (1258), ending the Abbasid Caliphate

figures.name: Ghazan Khan
figures.significance: Converted to Sunni Islam (1295) — the ideological turning point that fully Persianized the Ilkhanate

name: Timurid Empire
note: Timur was Barlas Turkic; court was thoroughly Persian — Samarkand and Herat became the greatest centers of Persian poetry and miniature painting

figures.name: Timur (Tamerlane)
figures.significance: Last great nomadic conqueror; made Samarkand the most dazzling city of the age

figures.name: Babur
figures.significance: Last Timurid; lost Samarkand and conquered India instead; founded the Mughal Empire

name: Safavid Empire
note: Established Twelver Shia as state religion; this religious identity is what NOW defines Iran as distinct from Sunni Turkic/Arab neighbors — same Persian ethnicity as the Timurids but a completely different civilizational identity

figures.name: Shah Ismail I
figures.significance: Forcibly converted Iran from Sunni to Shia Islam; created the civilizational identity that defines Iran today

figures.name: Shah Abbas I (The Great)
figures.significance: Rebuilt Isfahan; zenith of Safavid power and Persian art
